stem: What causes anatomy outside the selected field of view to appear on the opposite side of the image?

options.text: Undersampling that makes outside and inside positions indistinguishable
Excessive longitudinal recovery that changes tissue contrast during a long TR
Perfect refocusing that displaces spins after a 180-degree pulse
Oversampling produced by a field of view larger than the patient

explanation: When spatial frequencies are sampled below the Nyquist requirement, signals outside the FOV are assigned an incorrect in-field position. The result is aliasing or wraparound.

stem: Which adjustment directly reduces phase-direction wraparound?

options.text: Lengthen TE while keeping the phase sampling and field of view unchanged
Increase phase coverage by phase oversampling or a larger phase FOV
Reduce receiver bandwidth while leaving phase coverage unchanged
Increase slice thickness without changing the phase sampling interval

explanation: Phase oversampling acquires enough phase information to distinguish anatomy outside the nominal display FOV. A larger phase FOV can also include the anatomy but may reduce resolution unless matrix changes.

stem: Why is frequency-direction wraparound often easier to prevent than phase-direction wraparound?

options.text: Frequency encoding uses no spatial gradient and therefore needs no extra sampling
All anatomy has the same frequency during readout, preventing ambiguous positions
Extra readout samples fit within the same echo without extra sequence repetitions
Frequency oversampling reduces the number of receiver samples needed for reconstruction

explanation: Readout oversampling adds data points within an existing acquisition window and can be filtered before reconstruction. Additional phase samples usually require more repetitions and therefore more time.

stem: How does parallel imaging reconstruct data omitted by phase undersampling?

options.text: It assumes omitted k-space lines contain the same values as adjacent measured lines
It repeats the scan without gradients and substitutes that signal for phase data
It replaces missing phase data with a map of independently measured T1 values
It uses distinct spatial sensitivity profiles from multiple receiver coils

explanation: Array coils view anatomy with different sensitivity profiles, providing extra spatial information that can separate aliased signals. Reconstruction combines that information with the undersampled k-space data.

stem: What is a typical cost of increasing the parallel-imaging acceleration factor?

options.text: Lower SNR and greater risk of reconstruction artifact
Longer scan time from additional phase steps
Complete removal of coil sensitivity effects
Higher SNR because less data are acquired

explanation: Acceleration reduces the number of phase-encoding steps and shortens the scan, but less data and noise amplification reduce SNR. Calibration or coil-geometry limitations can create residual aliasing.
